original_hypothesis.title: MagicLP.sellBase/sellQuote use tx.origin for fee rate lookup enabling fee manipulation
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: sellBase and sellQuote pass tx.origin to querySellBase/querySellQuote for fee rate determination. If the FeeRateModel implementation discriminates by trader address, any contract calling these functions inherits the EOA's fee rate, not its own. This breaks composability and enables fee rate manipulation through intermediary contracts.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: Using tx.origin for fee-sensitive logic is a well-known anti-pattern. The deployed FeeRateModelImpl splits fees 50/50 regardless of trader, but the architecture allows the owner to change the implementation. A future implementation that uses per-trader rates would be exploitable.
deployment_assumptions: FeeRateModel implementation may be changed to one that discriminates by trader address
original_hypothesis.config_evidence: MIMSwap.s.sol deploys FeeRateModelImpl which ignores trader parameter, but FeeRateModel.setImplementation allows changing this at any time

proof_of_signal.pattern: querySellBase(tx.origin, baseInput) uses tx.origin instead of msg.sender for fee rate lookup

preconditions: FeeRateModel implementation differentiates fees by trader address
Swaps are executed through intermediary contracts (Router, aggregators)

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: tx.origin used as trader parameter in fee rate queries across sellBase, sellQuote, and flashLoan
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Intermediary contracts inherit EOA fee rate; smart contract wallets get inconsistent fee treatment
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: Should use msg.sender for fee rate determination

analysis_questions: What fee rate model is currently deployed and does it discriminate by trader?
Can an attacker route through a privileged EOA to get lower fees?
Does this affect Factory.create similarly (tx.origin as creator)?
validation_requirements: Deploy a FeeRateImpl that discriminates by trader
Verify that contract-based swaps inherit EOA fee rate
Test with Router swaps to confirm tx.origin propagation

original_hypothesis.preliminary_attack_scenario: If a future FeeRateModel discriminates by address (e.g., whitelisted addresses get lower mtFee), an attacker could route trades through an intermediary contract while using a whitelisted EOA as tx.origin, paying lower fees than intended for their actual trading entity.
prerequisites: Fee rate implementation that uses trader address for discrimination

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Reduced fee revenue for protocol; unfair fee distribution among traders
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: Protocol fee recipient and honest traders paying full fees
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Requires code change to use msg.sender instead of tx.origin

original_hypothesis.test_coverage_gap: No tests for fee rate with contract-based callers vs EOA callers

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Difficult to detect - fee rates appear legitimate per trader identity

validation_result.mechanism: tx.origin misuse in fee rate lookup
validation_result.impact_summary: sellBase, sellQuote, and flashLoan use tx.origin instead of msg.sender for fee rate queries, breaking composability and creating a latent vulnerability exploitable if the FeeRateModel implementation is changed to discriminate by trader address.
validation_result.detailed_analysis: The hypothesis correctly identifies that MagicLP.sellBase (line 208), sellQuote (line 224), and flashLoan (lines 252, 263) pass tx.origin to querySellBase/querySellQuote, which forwards it to _MT_FEE_RATE_MODEL_.getFeeRate(trader, _LP_FEE_RATE_). This is a well-known anti-pattern. The current deployed FeeRateModelImpl ignores the trader parameter entirely (splitting fees 50/50 regardless), so there is no immediate exploitable impact. However, the code is architecturally defective: it identifies the trader as the EOA rather than the direct caller, which (1) breaks composability with smart contract wallets and meta-transactions, (2) prevents reliable per-entity fee discrimination, and (3) creates a latent vulnerability if FeeRateModel.setImplementation is used to deploy a trader-discriminating fee model. The FeeRateModel owner can change the implementation at any time via setImplementation(), making this a real architectural risk.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: Lines 208-209 of MagicLP.sol clearly show `querySellBase(tx.origin, baseInput)`. Lines 224 show `querySellQuote(tx.origin, quoteInput)`. Lines 252 and 263 in flashLoan also use `tx.origin`. The querySellBase function at line 137 passes the trader to `_MT_FEE_RATE_MODEL_.getFeeRate(trader, _LP_FEE_RATE_)`. FeeRateModel.sol line 34 forwards this to `IFeeRateImpl(implementation).getFeeRate(msg.sender, trader, lpFeeRate)`. FeeRateModelImpl.sol ignores both pool and trader parameters. FeeRateModel.setImplementation (line 50) allows the owner to change the implementation at any time.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: tx.origin is passed as the trader parameter instead of msg.sender. When called through the Router or any intermediary contract, tx.origin resolves to the EOA, not the actual calling contract. This means fee rates are determined by the EOA identity rather than the direct caller.
Same tx.origin anti-pattern as sellBase. Fee rate is determined by the EOA originating the transaction rather than the direct caller (e.g., Router contract).
Both the sell-quote and sell-base branches within flashLoan use tx.origin for fee rate determination, applying the same vulnerability as direct swap functions.
The owner can change the FeeRateModel implementation at any time. If a new implementation discriminates by trader address, the tx.origin usage in MagicLP becomes immediately exploitable.

calling_sequence: 1. User calls Router.sellBaseTokensForTokens() which calls MagicLP.sellBase(to)
2. sellBase reads baseInput from balance delta (line 206-207)
3. sellBase calls querySellBase(tx.origin, baseInput) at line 208 - tx.origin is the EOA, not the Router
4. querySellBase calls _MT_FEE_RATE_MODEL_.getFeeRate(trader, _LP_FEE_RATE_) at line 137 with trader=tx.origin
5. FeeRateModel.getFeeRate forwards to IFeeRateImpl(implementation).getFeeRate(msg.sender, trader, lpFeeRate) at line 34
6. Current FeeRateModelImpl ignores both pool and trader parameters, returning 50/50 split
7. If implementation is changed to a discriminating model, the wrong address (tx.origin instead of msg.sender) determines fee rates
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: The vulnerability is in the data flow: sellBase → querySellBase(tx.origin, ...) → _MT_FEE_RATE_MODEL_.getFeeRate(tx.origin, ...). The tx.origin value propagates through to the fee rate model where it's used as the trader identity. When the Router (msg.sender) calls sellBase, tx.origin is the user's EOA, not the Router. Any future fee model that applies different rates based on trader address will apply the EOA's rate to the Router's swap, breaking the intended fee discrimination.

deployment_assumptions_checked: FeeRateModel implementation may be changed to one that discriminates by trader address
config_validation.deployment_evidence: MIMSwap.s.sol deploys FeeRateModelImpl which ignores the trader parameter (confirmed in FeeRateModelImpl.sol lines 10-16). However, FeeRateModel.setImplementation (FeeRateModel.sol:46-50) allows the owner to change the implementation at any time. The deployment script at MIMSwap.s.sol line 36-38 shows `feeRateModel.setImplementation(feeRateModelImpl)` being called, confirming this is a mutable configuration.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: With the current FeeRateModelImpl, the tx.origin usage has no exploitable impact because the trader parameter is ignored. However, the code defect is real: if the owner calls FeeRateModel.setImplementation() with a new model that offers lower rates to whitelisted addresses, an attacker could route trades through intermediary contracts while using a whitelisted EOA as tx.origin, paying lower fees than intended. The attack is straightforward: whitelisted EOA signs a transaction that calls through a malicious contract performing arbitrage swaps, all at the whitelisted rate. Smart contract wallets (e.g., Gnosis Safe) would also receive incorrect fee treatment since tx.origin would be the executor EOA rather than the Safe address.
validation_result.impact_assessment: Currently LOW impact due to the non-discriminating FeeRateModelImpl. If the fee model is changed to discriminate by trader address: MEDIUM impact - reduced protocol fee revenue, unfair fee distribution, and broken composability for smart contract wallets. The defect is architectural and affects all pools using the same FeeRateModel.
recommended_mitigations: Replace tx.origin with msg.sender in sellBase, sellQuote, and flashLoan fee rate queries
If EOA identification is intentionally needed for some future use case, add a separate parameter or use both msg.sender and tx.origin, letting the fee model decide which to use
Add integration tests that verify fee rates are correctly applied when swaps go through intermediary contracts vs direct EOA calls
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Deploy a custom FeeRateImpl that returns lower mtFee for address(0x1) and higher mtFee for all others. 2. Set this as the FeeRateModel implementation. 3. Call sellBase directly from address(0x1) and record the fee. 4. Deploy a contract that calls sellBase and have address(0x1) call that contract - verify the fee matches step 3 (it will, confirming tx.origin leaks). 5. Have address(0x2) call the same contract and verify the fee is different (it will be, but through the contract the fee should arguably be the contract's rate, not the EOA's).
uncertainties: Whether the tx.origin usage is an intentional design choice from DODO's original code or an oversight
Whether the protocol team plans to deploy a trader-discriminating fee model in the future
Whether the Factory.create tx.origin usage for salt computation is related to the same design philosophy
additional_testing_needed: Test fee rate behavior when swapping through Router vs direct MagicLP interaction with a discriminating fee model
Test smart contract wallet (e.g., Gnosis Safe) fee rate treatment
Verify the Factory.create tx.origin salt computation has similar composability issues
